script:
Hello, I am Josh Weinberg and today is Saturday, June 27, 2026 and The Portfolio Podcast QQQM update is as of June 27 2026 at 10:54 AM ET. The portfolio is sitting at ninety-eight thousand three hundred ninety-three dollars and fifty-two cents, down one point six one percent on the day, a loss of one thousand six hundred six dollars and forty-eight cents against a one hundred thousand dollar base. Not a clean week for tech-heavy positions.

Apple is seeking White House approval to buy memory chips from a blacklisted Chinese semiconductor company, and the market is not waiting for Washington's answer. Shares dropped hard this session. The geopolitical and supply chain implications stretch well beyond one ticker — they touch the entire AI hardware stack that underlies this portfolio. Stay with us.

No macro data dominates the tape today being a Saturday, but this week's broader theme was unmistakable: rotation. Money has been moving away from the AI hyperscaler trade and into sectors that had been left behind. Utilities, industrials, and energy-adjacent names absorbed some of the flow that was previously locked into mega-cap technology. For a portfolio weighted this heavily in tech and communication services, that rotation is a direct headwind, and today's numbers reflect exactly that.

The top story today is Apple, and it is layered. The Financial Times reported early this morning, confirmed by Bloomberg, that Apple is actively pressing the White House for a license to purchase memory chips from CXMT — ChangXin Memory Technologies — a Chinese firm that sits on the United States commerce department's entity list. Apple's interest is straightforward: cost containment. The AI memory market is extraordinarily tight right now, with Micron, Samsung, and SK hynix all operating near capacity constraints, and prices reflecting that scarcity. Apple wants an alternative. The problem is that CXMT is blacklisted for national security reasons, and any waiver would require a politically sensitive decision from the current administration. Markets read that as uncertainty, and uncertainty on supply chain combined with a separate Bloomberg report earlier this week noting that price hikes on iPads and Macs are already hitting consumer demand — that combination sent Apple down three point one seven percent today. At eight point nine seven percent of this QQQM portfolio, Apple is the single largest position. A session like this one costs the overall portfolio meaningful ground. What to watch: whether the White House responds, and whether any pushback on the Mac and iPad pricing filters into June retail data in the coming weeks.

Apple closed at two hundred eighty-three dollars and seventy-eight cents, down nine dollars and thirty cents, a loss of three point one seven percent on the session. Beyond the CXMT chip story, Yahoo Finance flagged a broader market narrative this morning: the AI boom rotation. Analysts are increasingly pointing to a baton-pass dynamic where hyperscaler-adjacent names like Apple face near-term multiple compression as capital cycles into other sectors. For the largest position in this portfolio, that is a meaningful structural headwind to monitor.

Turning to Microsoft, which was the one bright spot at the top of the weight table today. Microsoft closed at three hundred seventy-two dollars and ninety-seven cents, up seven dollars and fifty-one cents, a gain of two point zero five percent. The news flow here was constructive. Caterpillar's addition to the Russell Top 50 was tied directly to its record backlog in AI data center power generation, and the Chevron-Microsoft partnership for powering AI infrastructure was cited specifically. That narrative keeps Microsoft's cloud and AI capex story intact. Separately, a Bernstein note circulated this morning noted that while Google Cloud led in incremental AI cloud revenue in the first quarter of twenty-twenty-six, Microsoft's Azure trajectory remains steady with long-term growth intact. Microsoft at eight point four two percent of the portfolio was a partial offset to today's broader damage.

Over at NVIDIA, the session was painful. NVIDIA closed at one hundred ninety-two dollars and fifty-three cents, down six dollars and forty-seven cents, a decline of three point two five percent. The news tagged to NVIDIA today was largely mislabeled — stories about PepsiCo, Stellantis, and Qualcomm's edge AI push appeared in NVIDIA's feed but speak more to the broader semiconductor and AI infrastructure narrative. The Qualcomm edge AI story is worth noting contextually: as AI workloads migrate toward on-device processing, the data center GPU cycle that has been NVIDIA's core growth engine faces a longer-term question about concentration risk. For now, NVIDIA remains indispensable at the infrastructure layer, but a three point two five percent drop in a single session for the portfolio's third-largest position at eight point one five percent of the book is significant dollar drag.

Meanwhile, Amazon closed at two hundred thirty-two dollars and sixty-nine cents, down one dollar and fifty-eight cents, off zero point six seven percent. That relative outperformance is notable given the broader tech selloff. The news hook for Amazon today was Alphabet's addition to the Dow Jones Industrial Average, and an analyst piece speculating that SpaceX could be next — neither of which moves Amazon directly. Amazon Web Services remains the largest cloud provider by revenue, and the company's diversified revenue streams appear to be acting as a cushion on sessions like this one. At five point five two percent of the portfolio, the modest decline here helped limit overall damage.

Turning to Broadcom, which had the worst session of any holding today. Broadcom closed at three hundred sixty-five dollars and two cents, down seventeen dollars and five cents — a drop of four point four six percent. No company-specific news drove the move; this looks like sector-wide pressure on semiconductor names accelerating into the close. Broadcom's custom AI chip business for hyperscalers had been a significant driver of the stock's valuation expansion over the past eighteen months. When the hyperscaler trade rotates, Broadcom absorbs that pressure acutely. At four point six one percent of the portfolio, a four and a half percent down session leaves a mark.

Over at Meta Platforms, shares closed at five hundred fifty dollars and twenty-five cents, down seven dollars and forty-two cents, a loss of one point three three percent. The decline is in line with the broader communication services sector weakness today. No Meta-specific headlines crossed the tape, but the rotation narrative away from AI-adjacent mega-caps applies here. Meta's AI infrastructure investment cycle remains one of the most aggressive in the industry, and any sentiment shift on that trade touches this name directly.

Alphabet's Class A shares, GOOGL, closed at three hundred thirty-seven dollars and thirty-nine cents, down seven dollars and ninety cents, a decline of two point two nine percent. The Bernstein note that highlighted Google Cloud as the leading AI cloud platform in Q1 twenty-twenty-six was positive for the fundamental story, and Alphabet's addition to the Dow Jones Industrial Average this week reflects its standing. At four point one two percent of the portfolio, today's move is a drag but not outsized given the tape.

The Class C shares, GOOG, closed at three hundred thirty-four dollars and sixty-nine cents, down ten dollars and thirty-five cents, off three point zero zero percent. No company-specific news on the Class C shares today. The slight underperformance relative to Class A is not structurally meaningful — these are the same underlying business, different share classes. Combined, Alphabet represents just over eight percent of this portfolio.

Tesla closed at three hundred seventy-nine dollars and seventy-one cents, up four dollars and eighteen cents, a gain of one point one one percent. Along with Microsoft, Tesla was one of only two holdings to finish in positive territory today. No specific Tesla catalyst crossed the wire, but the stock's beta tends to work in both directions, and on a session where the broader market rotated away from AI infrastructure, Tesla's consumer discretionary exposure gave it a different correlation profile. At three point five four percent of the portfolio, the gain is modest but welcome.

Finally, Costco closed at nine hundred fifty-two dollars and fifty-four cents, down eight dollars and fifty-five cents, off zero point eight nine percent. A Yahoo Finance piece this morning actually cited Costco favorably, arguing the stock is worth every dollar near one thousand given its all-weather consumer appeal. The slight decline looks like sympathy selling rather than any Costco-specific deterioration. At two point eight one percent of the portfolio, this is the smallest weight in the top ten and had minimal impact on today's overall result.

Three numbers to watch over the next forty-eight hours. First, any White House response or leak regarding Apple's CXMT waiver request — if an answer surfaces over the weekend, Monday's open for AAPL moves sharply in either direction. Second, watch for any Fed speaker commentary on Sunday or Monday ahead of next week's economic data calendar — rate trajectory continues to influence mega-cap tech valuations directly. Third, keep an eye on any June consumer spending or retail data previews that surface Monday morning, which would give early read on whether Apple's iPad and Mac price hikes are showing up in demand erosion.

This is Josh Weinberg. The Portfolio Podcast returns at five PM Eastern on the next trading day. Overnight, the one variable to track is Washington — specifically any signal on that Apple chip waiver. If that story moves, the rest of this portfolio will follow.
